question: O and S are in Group 16. Explain why O is more electronegative than S.
full_answer: O is more electronegative than S because O has fewer occupied shells. This means less shielding and a smaller atomic radius. So the bonding pair of electrons is attracted more strongly to the nucleus.

question: Explain why Mg has a higher electrical conductivity than Na.
full_answer: Magnesium has a higher electrical conductivity than sodium because each magnesium atom contributes two delocalised electrons whereas each sodium atom contributes only one. Magnesium therefore has more mobile charge carriers.

question: Explain the trend in thermal stability of Group 2 carbonates.
full_answer: Down the group the M2+ ion has lower charge density. So it polarises the carbonate ion less. The carbonate ion is less distorted and is less likely to decompose.

question: Explain the trend in solubility of the Group 2 sulfates.
full_answer: The solubility of the Group 2 sulfates decreases down the group because both lattice enthalpy and hydration enthalpy become less exothermic. The hydration enthalpy decreases by a greater amount, so dissolution becomes less favourable.

question: Explain why the yield of a gaseous product increases when the total pressure is increased.
full_answer: Increasing pressure shifts the equilibrium to the side with fewer moles of gas. Therefore the equilibrium moves to the product side if that side has fewer gaseous molecules, so the yield increases.

question: Explain why increasing temperature increases the rate of reaction.
full_answer: At a higher temperature the particles have greater average kinetic energy. A greater proportion of particles has energy greater than or equal to the activation energy. Therefore the number of successful collisions per unit time increases.

full_answer: A catalyst increases the rate of reaction by providing an alternative pathway with a lower activation energy. A greater proportion of collisions is therefore successful.

question: Explain why propan-2-ol is the major product when propene reacts by electrophilic addition.
full_answer: The secondary carbocation is more stable than the primary carbocation. This is because two alkyl groups have a greater positive inductive effect than one alkyl group. Therefore the pathway through the secondary carbocation is favoured.

question: Explain why the yield of hydrogen iodide is low when NaI reacts with concentrated sulfuric acid.
full_answer: Iodide ions are strong reducing agents. The hydrogen iodide formed is oxidised by concentrated sulfuric acid to iodine. So some hydrogen iodide is used up and the yield is low.

question: Explain why a chloro compound shows both an M peak and an M+2 peak.
full_answer: Chlorine has two common isotopes 35Cl and 37Cl. Molecules containing 37Cl have a mass two units greater than molecules containing 35Cl. So both an M peak and an M+2 peak are seen.

question: Explain why CO has a higher boiling point than N2.
full_answer: CO is a polar molecule and N2 is non-polar. CO has permanent dipole-dipole attractions in addition to instantaneous dipole-induced dipole attractions. Therefore the intermolecular forces in CO are stronger, so its boiling point is higher.

full_answer: Al3+ and Mg2+ are isoelectronic. Al3+ has more protons, so there is a stronger attraction between the nucleus and the electrons. Therefore Al3+ has the smaller radius.

question: Explain why HI gives a lower yield than HCl when sodium halides react with concentrated sulfuric acid.
full_answer: Iodide ions are stronger reducing agents than chloride ions. HI is oxidised by concentrated sulfuric acid, while HCl is not oxidised. Therefore some HI is used up in a redox reaction, so the yield of hydrogen halide is lower.

question: Explain the difference in the melting points of SiO2 and SiCl4 with reference to structure and bonding.
full_answer: SiO2 has a giant covalent structure with many strong covalent bonds that must be broken. SiCl4 is simple molecular and only weak intermolecular forces are overcome on melting. Therefore SiO2 has the much higher melting point.

question: Explain why iodide ions are stronger reducing agents than chloride ions.
full_answer: Iodide ions lose electrons more readily than chloride ions. Iodide ions are larger, so the outer electrons are further from the nucleus and are less strongly attracted. Therefore iodide ions are more easily oxidised and are stronger reducing agents.

question: Explain why a molecule containing one chlorine atom has an M peak and an M+2 peak in the mass spectrum.
full_answer: Chlorine has two common isotopes, 35Cl and 37Cl. Molecules containing 37Cl have a mass two units greater than molecules containing 35Cl, so an M+2 peak is observed as well as the M peak.
